1: A revelation of God: Psalm 60 is a communal lament after military defeat — historically connected to the Aramean campaigns of 2 Samuel 8 and 10. "O God, you have rejected us and broken our defenses; you have been angry — restore us!" The experience of divine rejection in military defeat is the covenant community's crisis. The NT community's lament is structured similarly: Romans 8:36 (quoting Psalms 44:22: "For your sake we face death all day long") — the apparent rejection by God is reframed in the resurrection. The restoration prayer ("restore us") is answered in Christ who takes the covenant people through apparent rejection to vindication.
2: A revelation of God: "You have made the earth shake; you have cracked it open; heal its fractures, for it is collapsing." The earthquake of divine judgment on the covenant community — the ground splitting under them — echoes the creation accounts where the earth is the arena of divine action. At the crucifixion, Matthew 27:51: "the earth shook, the rocks split." The earthquake of Psalms 60:2 is a type of the cosmic disturbance of the cross — the moment when YHWH's judgment-and-salvation falls in a way that shakes the earth literally. The prayer "heal its fractures" is answered in the new creation (Revelation 21:5: "I am making everything new!").
3: A theme: "You have put your people through severe trials; you have made us drink disorienting wine." The wine of disorientation — the divine cup of judgment that produces incoherence — is the Passion's own language. Mark 14:36: "Take this cup from me." John 18:11: "Shall I not drink the cup the Father has given me?" Christ drinks the cup of divine judgment that the covenant people could not bear — 2 Corinthians 5:21: "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us." The "disorienting wine" of Psalms 60:3 is the cup that Christ drinks so his people do not have to.
4: A direct connection: "You have set up a banner (nēs) for those who fear you, for them to rally to in the face of the enemy's bow." The battle-banner or standard (nēs) raised for the fearers to rally around is a theme that runs through Isaiah: Psalms 11:12 ("He will raise a banner for the nations and gather the exiles of Israel"), Psalms 49:22 ("I will lift up my banner to the peoples"). Isaiah 11:10 uses the same term for the Messianic Root of Jesse as the banner for the nations — cited by Paul in Romans 15:12. Most directly: John 3:14, "Just as Moses lifted up the snake in the desert, so the Son of Man must be lifted up" — Christ crucified is the nēs raised for all who fear God to rally around.
5: A revelation of God: "Save your dear ones — rescue them by your right hand, and answer our prayer." The prayer for salvation "by your right hand" is the Exodus language: Exodus 15:6, "Your right hand, LORD, was majestic in power; your right hand, LORD, shattered the enemy." Acts 2:33: "Exalted to the right hand of God, he has received from the Father the promised Holy Spirit." The "right hand" that saves is the right hand where Christ now sits — the one who is both the agent of the right-hand rescue and the one who occupies that position of power on behalf of his people.
6: A revelation of God: "God has spoken in his holiness: 'I will triumph; I will divide up Shechem and portion out the Valley of Succoth.'" The divine oracle of triumph — YHWH declaring victory over the territories — is the covenant-land speech pattern. The NT applies the territorial-triumph language eschatologically: Revelation 21:1-4 (the new heaven and earth), and Matthew 28:18 ("all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me"). The "I will triumph" of the divine oracle is fulfilled in Christ who has been given all authority — the triumph over the nations that Psalms 60:6 announces is his.
7: A direct connection: "'Gilead is mine, Manasseh is mine; Ephraim is my helmet; Judah is my scepter.'" "Judah is my scepter (mĕḥōqĕqî)" — the lawgiver/commander's staff belonging to Judah — directly echoes Genesis 49:10: "The scepter will not depart from Judah, nor the ruler's staff from between his feet, until he to whom it belongs shall come and the obedience of the nations shall be his." Revelation 5:5: "See, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has triumphed." Christ is the one to whom the scepter of Judah belongs — the fulfillment of Jacob's blessing and the divine oracle of Psalms 60:7.
8: A shadow: "'Moab is my washbasin; over Edom I throw my sandal; over Philistia I shout in triumph.'" The dismissive, humiliating language about the surrounding enemies — Moab as a basin, Edom receiving a thrown sandal — is the imagery of total suzerainty. The nations around Israel that opposed the Davidic kingdom are types of all powers hostile to the Messianic reign. Revelation 19:15: "He will rule them with an iron scepter. He treads the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God Almighty." The contemptuous dominion of Psalms 60:8 is the eschatological dominion of Christ over all hostile powers.
9: A theme: "Who will bring me into the fortified city? Who will guide me all the way to Edom?" The military question — what human commander can conquer the impregnable fortress? — is the admission of human inadequacy before the task. John 15:5: "apart from me you can do nothing." The community that cannot take the fortified city by its own power needs a guide and captain. Hebrews 2:10 calls Christ the "pioneer of their salvation" — the archēgos who leads the community through what they cannot accomplish themselves. The guide to Edom is the one who has already gone ahead.
10: A revelation of God: "Have you not rejected us, O God? You no longer go out, God, with our armies." The apparent divine absence from the covenant community's military struggles is the lament at the center of Psalm 60. This is the "dark night" of the Psalm — God seems to have abandoned the enterprise. Romans 8:31-32: "If God is for us, who can be against us? He who did not spare his own Son, but gave him up for us all — how will he not also, along with him, graciously give us all things?" The apparent abandonment of God going out with the armies is resolved in Christ: God's "going out" with the covenant community is now his going out in Christ, through the Spirit.
11: A revelation of God: "Give us help against the enemy, for human deliverance is worthless." The definitive confession of human inability — "human deliverance is worthless (šāwʾ tĕšûʿat ʾādām)" — is the OT anti-humanism that grounds the necessity of divine salvation. Acts 4:12: "Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved." The "worthless" human deliverance of Psalms 60:11 is the backdrop against which the name of Jesus stands out as the one non-human, non-worthless salvation available. Christ alone is the help against the enemy that no human power can supply.
12: A revelation of God: "Through God we will do great deeds; he himself will crush our enemies." The final declaration of divine-human cooperation — "through God" (bēʾlōhîm) great deeds, but YHWH himself doing the crushing — is the Christological framework of the NT. Philippians 4:13: "I can do all this through him who gives me strength." Colossians 2:15: "And having disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross." The "through God we do great deeds / he himself crushes our enemies" of Psalms 60:12 is the cross: the community works through Christ; Christ crushes the enemies by his own action at Golgotha.
